1: The extended oracle against Babylon and the land of the Chaldeans — the longest single oracle in Jeremiah — begins with YHWH speaking against the world's dominant power. Revelation 17-18 identifies Babylon as the symbolic name for the anti-God world-system that persists through all ages. The fall of historical Babylon is both a literal prophecy and a type: the ultimate Babylon is any civilization that sets itself up as the absolute authority against YHWH. Christ's return brings the definitive fall of every Babylon (Revelation 18:2, 10, 21).
2: The public proclamation — declare among the nations and proclaim; set up a banner and proclaim; do not conceal it — Babylon's fall is to be announced to all the nations. The proclamation of Babylon's fall in Revelation 14:8 ("fallen, fallen is Babylon the great") echoes this command. The gospel is also a public proclamation — not concealed but announced openly to all nations (Matthew 28:19; Luke 24:47). The good news of Babylon's fall is inseparable from the good news of Christ's victory, for both declare that the anti-God power will not have the last word.
3: The people from the north coming against Babylon to make the land a desolation — the same directional language used of Babylon coming against Israel — establishes a kind of poetic justice: what Babylon did to others will be done to Babylon. Paul's "do not be deceived: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap" (Galatians 6:7) is the NT principle underlying this reversal. The justice that Christ embodies is the justice of perfect accountability: no system built on oppression escapes the accounting.
4: The promise — in those days and in that time the people of Israel and the people of Judah shall come together, weeping as they go, and seek YHWH their God — is the eschatological reunion of the divided kingdom in repentance. Jesus prays for the unity of his followers: "that they may be one, as we are one" (John 17:22). Ephesians 2:11-22 presents Christ as the one who has broken down the dividing wall — uniting not just Israel and Judah but Jew and Gentile into one new humanity. The eschatological unity of the covenant people is accomplished in Christ.
5: The people seeking YHWH — asking the way to Zion with faces turned toward it: come, let us join ourselves to YHWH in an everlasting covenant that will never be forgotten — is the pilgrimage of the restored people to covenant renewal. Jesus is "the way" (John 14:6) — he himself is the path to Zion, the access to the Father. The "everlasting covenant that will never be forgotten" is the new covenant whose blood is Christ's (Luke 22:20; Hebrews 13:20) — permanent, unforgettable, and secured in the resurrection.
6: The diagnosis — my people have been lost sheep; their shepherds have led them astray, turning them away on the mountains; they have gone from mountain to hill, they have forgotten their resting place — is the precise condition that Jesus describes the crowd as being in: "when he saw the crowds, he had compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd" (Matthew 9:36). Jesus presents himself as the response: "I am the good shepherd" (John 10:11). The lost sheep of Jeremiah 50 are the lost sheep Jesus seeks until he finds them (Luke 15:4-7).
7: The oppressors' excuse — all who found them have devoured them; their enemies said: we are not guilty, for they have sinned against YHWH, their true habitation — is the logic of blaming the victim for their covenant failure. The NT addresses this: Jesus does not accept the disciples' assumption that the man born blind sinned or that his parents did (John 9:3). The sinner's vulnerability does not license oppression; rather, it intensifies the responsibility to show mercy. Christ's atonement addresses sin without using it as justification for oppression.
8: The command to flee Babylon — flee from the midst of Babylon, and go out of the land of the Chaldeans — is the call to separation from the corrupt system. Revelation 18:4: "come out of her, my people, lest you take part in her sins, lest you share in her plagues." This is the NT application of Jeremiah 50:8 — the command to the people of God to not be conformed to the world-system (Romans 12:2). The call out of Babylon is the call to the alternative community shaped by the gospel.
9: The great nations from the north stirred up against Babylon — their arrows are like a skilled warrior who does not return empty-handed — is the divine mobilization of history's powers as instruments of judgment. Paul in Romans 13:1-4 describes political power as "God's servant for your good" and as one who "carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer." The nations that YHWH uses as instruments of judgment are under his direction — an expression of Christ's lordship over the political powers that Paul insists are "instituted by God."
10: Babylon plundered — Chaldea shall be plundered; all who plunder her shall be satisfied — is the reversal of the plunderer: the one who stripped nations is itself stripped. The Beatitude "blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy" (Matthew 5:7) has its dark complement: those who show no mercy will receive none. Babylon's plundering of nations returns on its own head — the measure-for-measure justice that the NT calls the divine judgment running through history.
11: YHWH's address to the plunderers of Israel — though you rejoice, though you exult, O plunderers of my heritage, though you frolic like a heifer in pasture — is the divine word against those who rejoiced in Israel's suffering. The NT locates the final reckoning for this in the parable of the sheep and goats (Matthew 25:31-46): "I was hungry and you did not feed me... as you did not do it to one of the least of these, you did not do it to me." Indifference to or rejoicing in the suffering of God's people is treated as treatment of Christ himself.
12: Babylon's mother being utterly shamed, she who bore you being put to shame — the image of Babylon's founding civilization brought to shame at its heir's destruction. The NT uses shame language for the cross: Christ "endured the cross, despising the shame" (Hebrews 12:2), transforming shame itself. The eschatological reversal is that those who trust in Christ "will not be put to shame" (Romans 10:11; 1 Peter 2:6), while those who built their systems on oppression inherit the shame they tried to avoid.
13: Babylon becoming a waste, a dry land, a desert; no one shall dwell there — the permanent desolation of the world's greatest city. Revelation 18:21-23 uses similar imagery: Babylon thrown down "shall be found no more." The contrast with the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2-3) is the contrast between the city of man built on opposition to God and the city of God descending from heaven. The desolation of one is the precondition for the flourishing of the other; Christ's judgment of Babylon is inseparable from his establishment of the new order.
14: The call to draw the bow against Babylon — all you who bend the bow, shoot at her, spare no arrows, for she has sinned against YHWH — frames the military attack as covenant justice. The cosmic battle in Revelation (Jeremiah 19:11-21) is described similarly: the rider on the white horse, whose name is the Word of God, strikes the nations. The "sword from his mouth" (Revelation 19:15) is the divine word executing judgment — the bow of the nations is replaced by the word of Christ, which is the instrument of both judgment and salvation.
15: Babylon's walls falling — her walls have fallen; it is the vengeance of YHWH. Take vengeance on her; as she has done, do to her — is the covenant-justice principle applied to the empire. Paul addresses vengeance carefully: "Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord" (Romans 12:19, citing Deuteronomy 32:35). The divine vengeance that Jeremiah announces is precisely what Paul says belongs to God alone — and what Christ executes at the end, freeing his people from the need to exact it themselves.
16: The cutting off of the sower and the one who handles the sickle in harvest time — the disruption of agricultural production as the practical sign of societal collapse. Against this agricultural death, the NT promises an eschatological harvest: "the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few" (Matthew 9:37); "the Son of Man will send his angels, and they will gather out of his kingdom all causes of sin" (Matthew 13:41). The sower and harvester cut off by judgment will be replaced by the divine sower who plants the word and the angels who gather the harvest.
17: Israel as a hunted sheep driven away by lions — first by Assyria and then Babylon — is the image of the covenant people as prey. Jesus presents himself as the counterforce: the Good Shepherd who gives his life for the sheep rather than fleeing from the wolf (John 10:11-13). The lion that scattered Israel is overcome by the Lion of Judah who gathers his scattered flock (Revelation 5:5; John 10:16). The hunted sheep imagery is resolved in Christ who both absorbs the attack of the predator and becomes the protector.
18: YHWH's declaration that I am bringing punishment on the king of Babylon and his land as I brought punishment on the king of Assyria — the consistent pattern of divine judgment on imperial powers. Paul's argument in Romans 9:17 uses Pharaoh to make the same point: God raised up Pharaoh precisely to demonstrate divine power. Each imperial power — Pharaoh, Assyria, Babylon — is used in the divine economy and then judged. Christ's lordship over all empires (Revelation 1:5: "ruler of kings on earth") is the NT statement of what Jeremiah demonstrates historically.
19: The promise of Israel's return — I will restore Israel to his pasture and he shall feed on Carmel and Bashan — is the eschatological restoration of the people to their inheritance. Paul's argument in Romans 11:26 ("all Israel will be saved") invokes this restoration trajectory. The feeding on Carmel and Bashan — the richest pastures of the promised land — is the OT image of covenant satiation that the NT presents as the eschatological feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Matthew 8:11).
20: The most sweeping forgiveness promise in Jeremiah 50 — in those days and at that time... the iniquity of Israel shall be sought and there shall be none, and the sins of Judah, and they shall not be found, for I will pardon those whom I leave as a remnant — directly echoes the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:34 ("I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more"). Hebrews 10:17-18 quotes 31:34 as the proof that Christ's sacrifice has accomplished permanent forgiveness. The iniquity sought and not found is the condition Hebrews declares accomplished in the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 9:26; Jeremiah 10:14).
21: YHWH's command against Merathaim (double bitterness) and Pekod (visitation) — symbolic names for Babylon — frames the judgment as divinely commanded, not merely political happenstance. The symbolic names point to the theological character of the judgment: Babylon's double bitterness (the bitterness it caused and the bitterness of its own judgment) and its divine visitation. Christ's coming is described as "the visitation" (episkopē, Luke 19:44; 1 Peter 2:12) — divine attention that either saves or judges depending on the response.
22: The sound of war and great destruction in the land — the battle language of Babylon's fall — is the language of eschatological war. Revelation 19:11-21 presents the eschatological battle in the same terms. The "great destruction" is the destruction of all that opposes the reign of God — the final clearing away of every Babylon so that the New Jerusalem can come down. Between the historical fall of Babylon and the eschatological fall stands Christ's cross, which is the decisive victory that makes the final reckoning certain.
23: The taunt — how the hammer of the whole earth is cut down and broken! How Babylon has become a desolation among the nations! — describes the fall of the universal instrument of power. Paul uses hammer/weapon imagery in 2 Corinthians 10:4: "the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds." The hammer of the whole earth is broken so that the divine power — working through apparent weakness — can accomplish what no earthly force could. Christ crucified is God's foolishness that is wiser than human wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:25).
24: Babylon caught in a trap without its knowledge — the judgment coming upon the self-confident empire by surprise. Jesus repeatedly warns against the trap of spiritual complacency: "be on guard, keep awake. For you do not know when the time will come" (Mark 13:33); "like a trap it will come upon all who dwell on the face of the whole earth" (Luke 21:35). The surprise of judgment for the spiritually careless is the consistent NT warning, resolved only by the watchfulness of those who know the Bridegroom is coming.
25: YHWH opening his armory and bringing out the weapons of his wrath — the divine arsenal deployed against Babylon. The armor of God passage in Ephesians 6:10-18 reappropriates the divine-warrior imagery from the OT prophets: the same divine armory that executes judgment on Babylon becomes the armor given to the church for spiritual warfare. Christ is both the warrior who wields YHWH's weapons in judgment and the one who arms his people to "stand against the schemes of the devil."
26: The command — come against her from every quarter; open her granaries; pile her up like heaps of grain and devote her to destruction; let nothing be left of her — is the total destruction (ḥerem) applied to the empire that had applied it to others. The total-dedication language (ḥerem) runs from the conquest of Canaan to the final judgment. Revelation 20:14-15 uses the image of the lake of fire for the complete and permanent removal of death, Hades, and all whose names are not in the Book of Life — the ultimate ḥerem that ends the reign of evil permanently.
27: The slaughter of Babylon's warriors — kill all her bulls; let them go down to the slaughter — is the divine purging of the power structures built on oppression. The "bulls" as the powerful leaders echoes Psalms 22:12 ("strong bulls of Bashan encircle me") — the image of oppressive power surrounding the suffering righteous one. Christ, the one surrounded by the bulls on the cross, is vindicated; the bulls that surround him face the slaughter he faced on their behalf.
28: The fugitives from Babylon declaring in Zion the vengeance of YHWH our God, the vengeance for his temple — the proclamation of divine justice from within the experience of deliverance. Revelation 18:20 ("rejoice over her, O heaven, and you saints and apostles and prophets, for God has given judgment for you against her") is the NT declaration of the same divine justice. The vengeance for the temple — for everything Babylon destroyed — is announced not from bitterness but from the perspective of those who have been rescued.
29: The summoning of archers against Babylon — let none escape; repay her according to her deeds; do to her according to all that she has done — is the divine insistence on complete moral accountability. The "according to their deeds" formula appears in Revelation 20:12 at the final judgment. The moral ledger that Babylon accumulated is precisely the ledger that the gospel addresses: Christ takes the ledger of sin (Colossians 2:14, the "record of debt") and cancels it at the cross. The accountability Babylon faces is the accountability from which those in Christ are freed.
30: The prophecy — her young men shall fall in her squares, and all her soldiers shall be destroyed in that day — is the military annihilation of Babylon's power. The consistent NT theme is that all human military and political power is temporary and will be overturned: "he has put down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate" (Luke 1:52). The fall of Babylon's soldiers is the historical type of the eschatological reversal of all power built on violence and oppression.
31: The direct address — behold, I am against you, O most proud one, declares the Lord God of hosts, for your day has come, the time when I will punish you — is YHWH's direct confrontation of pride. The NT presents Christ as the direct confrontation of all human pride: his cross is "a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles" (1 Corinthians 1:23) precisely because it inverts the values of power and wisdom that pride celebrates. The one who is against the proud is the one who welcomes the humble — and Christ's cross is the place where this divine disposition is most fully expressed.
32: The proud stumbling and falling with no one to raise him up — in contrast to YHWH's promise to raise up Israel. Acts 3:15 and 4:10 make the raising-up contrast central: Israel killed the Author of life but God raised him from the dead. Those who refuse the one who raises the dead are left with no one to raise them. The raising-up that Babylon cannot receive is the resurrection gift that Christ offers — the ultimate reversal of every fall, available only through the one who was himself raised.
33: The solidarity of oppression — the people of Israel and Judah together have been oppressed; all who took them captive have held them fast, they refuse to let them go — is the image of captivity under Babylon's power. The Exodus language ("refuse to let them go") deliberately echoes Pharaoh's refusal. Paul identifies this captivity in cosmic terms: humanity held captive under sin (Galatians 3:22), under the elemental spirits (Galatians 4:3), under the power of darkness (Colossians 1:13). Christ's redemption is the new Exodus: "he has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son" (Colossians 1:13).
34: Their Redeemer is strong; YHWH of hosts is his name — one of the most direct christological titles in the OT prophets. The goʾel (Redeemer) who is strong (ḥazaq) is the kinsman-redeemer who has the power to execute the right of redemption. The NT applies this: "since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death" (Hebrews 2:14). Christ becomes the strong Redeemer by entering the captivity, paying the ransom, and rising victorious.
35: The sword against the Chaldeans and the inhabitants of Babylon — the comprehensive judgment striking every level of Babylonian society. The "sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" (Ephesians 6:17) is the NT reappropriation of the divine-sword imagery: the same divine word that executes judgment on Babylon becomes the gospel proclamation that is "living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword" (Hebrews 4:12). The sword of judgment and the word of life are the same divine reality operating in different modes.
36: The sword against her diviners, that they may be made fools — Babylon's famous wise men and astrologers exposed as fools by the divine judgment. Isaiah 47:13-15 addresses Babylon's astrologers and wise counselors with the same exposure. Paul applies the same logic to the "wise men, the scribes, the debater of this age" (1 Corinthians 1:20): "God has made foolish the wisdom of this world." The exposure of Babylon's diviners is the OT type of the cross as the exposure of all human wisdom that does not know God (1 Corinthians 1:21).
37: The sword against her horses and chariots and all the foreign troops in her midst who will become women — the military feminization that represents total defeat. The NT inverts the military image: Christ's power "is made perfect in weakness" (2 Corinthians 12:9); the power that conquers is not military might but the word of the cross. The horses and chariots that represent Babylon's military confidence are replaced by the white horse of the Word of God (Revelation 19:11-13), whose name is written on him: King of kings and Lord of lords.
38: The sword/drought against her waters, that they may be dried up — the disruption of Babylon's water supply (the Euphrates). Revelation 16:12 describes the drying up of the Euphrates to prepare the way for the kings from the east in the eschatological judgment. Against the drying waters of judgment, Jesus offers the water that never runs dry: "the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life" (John 4:14). The drying of Babylon's waters contrasts with the ever-flowing river of the water of life from the throne of God and the Lamb (Revelation 22:1).
39: Babylon becoming a haunt of jackals, a home of ostriches — the wilderness creatures that inherit the desolate city. This is the fate of the anti-God civilization: Isaiah 13:21-22 and Revelation 18:2 ("Babylon has become a dwelling place for demons") use similar imagery. Against the habitation of the desolate city by unclean creatures, the New Jerusalem is inhabited by those whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life (Revelation 21:27) — the contrast between the habitation of death and the habitation of eternal life.
40: The Sodom-comparison — as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah and their neighbor cities, no man shall dwell there, no son of man shall sojourn in her — makes Babylon's destruction the ultimate expression of covenant judgment. Jesus uses the Sodom comparison for the judgment on cities that reject the gospel (Matthew 10:15; Jeremiah 11:23-24), establishing Sodom as the benchmark for judgment on those who reject the divine visitation. The fall of Babylon calibrated to Sodom's destruction is the most severe possible statement of what awaits all anti-God civilization at the end.
41: The people from the north — a great nation and many kings are stirring from the farthest parts of the earth — coming against Babylon inverts the pattern of Babylon coming against Israel from the north. The eschatological reversal of the north-invader motif (also in Ezek 38-39 and Revelation 20:8) frames the judgment on Babylon as the divine reversal of all military aggression. Christ's return is described in Revelation 19:11-16 as the ultimate northern invader from heaven — the one who comes not to oppress the weak but to judge the oppressors.
42: The invaders described as cruel and without mercy, their sound is like the roaring sea — the instruments of judgment portrayed in terrifying terms. The "roaring sea" image recurs in Revelation (the sea of glass, the voice of many waters) and in Jesus's description of the end: "the sea and the waves roaring" as signs of the Son of Man's coming (Luke 21:25-27). The terrifying noise of judgment is the opposite of the "still, small voice" of grace — both are divine; the difference is in the posture of the hearer.
43: The king of Babylon hearing the report about them and his hands falling limp; anguish taking hold of him, pain as of a woman in labor — the mightiest monarch in the world reduced to birth-pangs of terror. Luke 21:26 speaks of "people fainting with fear and with foreboding of what is coming on the world" in the description of the end times. The king who made nations tremble is himself made to tremble — the great reversal of power that Christ's return enacts completely and permanently.
44: YHWH like a lion coming up from the thicket of the Jordan against a strong sheepfold — the same image used of judgment against Edom in Jeremiah 49:19. The repeated lion-coming-up image in the OAN section establishes YHWH as the sovereign predator who holds all nations accountable. Revelation 5:5 ("the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered") applies this imagery to Christ: the Lion who judged the nations is the Lion who conquered on behalf of his sheep. The predator becomes the protector in the great christological reversal.
45: The appeal — hear the plan of YHWH against Babylon, and the purposes he has formed against the land of the Chaldeans — frames the oracle as the communication of the divine plan (machashevet). Ephesians 3:10-11 speaks of "the eternal purpose that he has realized in Christ Jesus our Lord" — the divine plan that was hidden and is now made known. The same word (boulē, counsel/plan) describes both the divine plan for Babylon's judgment and the divine plan for redemption: both are expressions of the same sovereign divine purposefulness.
46: The climactic cry — at the sound of the capture of Babylon the earth shall tremble, and her cry shall be heard among the nations — is the eschatological earthquake that marks the end of the anti-God empire. Revelation 18:9-10 describes the kings of the earth weeping at the fall of Babylon: "Alas, alas, you great city, you mighty city, Babylon! For in a single hour your judgment has come." The earth-shaking that accompanies the fall of every Babylon — historical, spiritual, and final — is the trembling that precedes the establishment of the unshakeable kingdom (Hebrews 12:28), whose builder and maker is God.
